issue_title: SNMPV3 AES192/256 privacy does not work with Cisco IOS.
issue_body:
Pysnmp SNMPV3 AES192/AES256 privacy options does not work for Cisco devices 
(test this with any Cisco device running an IOS that suppports AES192/AES256).

This is because Cisco devices (ie, IOS that supports AES192/AES256) do not use 
https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-blumenthal-aes-usm-04 for key localization/short key extension.
Instead, they use the procedure for 3DES key localization specified in
 https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-reeder-snmpv3-usm-3desede-00 which
specifies the 3DES key localization/short key extension. 
I believe Cisco did this because they view the reeder mechanism as an improvement 
over the bluementhal mechanism.

Please see the pull request that fixes this issue. However, the pull breaks
SNMPV3 AES192/AES256 privacy for any device that uses the key localization in 
https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-blumenthal-aes-usm-04

issue_title: Powers treated differently to other operators regarding whitespace correction
issue_body:
I am new to git and programming and got a bit confused by the PEP 8 style guide, so sorry if this is incorrect.

Using autopep8, it appears that powers are treated a bit differently to other operators, particularly if there is no whitespace.

In this example, I will use a hyphen to indicate a space, as multiple spaces are removed on here.

Both `2*2` and `2---*---2` will be corrected to `2-*-2`

Is this intentional? It seems a bit inconsistent that for other operators the whitespace will be set to one space regardless of how much whitespace was added, whereas for powers the whitespace will only be set to one space if there was any whitespace added beforehand.

Thank you very much for your help

[Note: This isn't an immediate issue, but I want to jot down some thoughts here for future reference.]

The `hierarchy.tmeasure` implementation involves a [summation](https://github.com/craffel/mir_eval/blob/master/mir_eval/hierarchy.py#L146) that takes `O(n * w**2)` time, where `n` is the number of frames and `w` is the window length.  This calculation derives from counting the number of disordered triples `(q, i, j) || (q, j, i)` between estimate and reference.  We use a naive algorithm to do this via numpy ufuncs.

For small values of `w`, this is acceptably fast.  (There are other reasons to use small `w` for t-measures, as we describe in the paper, so this doesn't really come up in practice yet.)  However, when `w` is large -- or in the case of a new metric I'm developing for label hierarchies, `w==n` -- this approaches cubic time and is generally bad news.

This calculation can be reduced to `O(n w log(w))` with a divide-and-conquer algorithm for inversion counting.  For large `w`, this becomes `O(n^2 log(n))`, which is within a `log(n)` factor of the other frame-wise segmentation metrics (nce, pairwise-f, etc).

I'm implementing this enhancement in the fork that includes the label hierarchy measures.  Unless there are objections, I'll just include it with that PR, but we could also do it separately if there's sufficient need.

issue_title: Output is not colored on Windows when redirecting standard output to a file
issue_body:
Hi there!

Thanks for this awesome project :-)

I've recently hit a case where I redirected the standard output to a file and all logs to standard error get these strange symbols:
```
←[33mWARNING←[0m:←[1mroot←[0m:←[33mApplied some throttling!←[0m

It seems like colorama doesn't detect that there is a tty when only the standard error stream is a tty.

I started digging through the code and noticed this:
```
# colorama/win32.py

def winapi_test():
    handle = handles[STDOUT]
    csbi = CONSOLE_SCREEN_BUFFER_INFO()
    success = _GetConsoleScreenBufferInfo(
        handle, byref(csbi))
    return bool(success)
```

If I change the code to this, then it works for me:
```
def _winapi_test(handle):
    csbi = CONSOLE_SCREEN_BUFFER_INFO()
    success = _GetConsoleScreenBufferInfo(
        handle, byref(csbi))
    return bool(success)

def winapi_test():
    return any(_winapi_test(h) for h in handles.values())
```

Any chance you're willing to accept a patch for this?

Thanks!

Related to #123

At present, the following code works:
```
NN1 = NNCorrelation(config)
NN1 += NN2
```
where DD is some other NN correlation object. This works because the "+=" operator (__iadd__) has been overloaded starting on line 256 of nncorrelation.py. However, when creating jackknifes this is not all we need.

To do jackknifing intelligently, we need to be able to have an overloaded "-=" and be able to multiply correlations. Essentially, we also need:
```
NN1 -= NN2
NN1 += 0.5 * NN2
```
This amounts to implementing functions very similar to __iadd__. If you want I do this myself and issue a pull request.

issue_title: Child Rows hidden on 1st Page when Items Per Page is set to 1 (one)
issue_body:
Admittedly this is an extreme case, but it is too odd not to report.

Currently using Tablesorter 2.23.5.

I am working on a small sample category-based data set for the same project I've been working on. As such, I set the table pager to one (1) item per page. Currently, the data set has two (2) top-level "parent" categories. Each parent has several subrows (i.e., child rows). Yep - I'm doing a depth stress test. 

The result was unexpected. With the pager set to only 1 (which in real life would probably NEVER be done) the first parent category "lost" all of its children. However, the second parent category which appears on page 2 is perfectly fine. 

I took a peek in the Chrome dev window and saw that all of the children of the 1st parent category had been set to "display:none;" I even toggled the value of countChildRows to see if there was an effect, but it made no difference.

Just on a hunch, I set the pager to 2 items per page and everything is now performing as it should. I even added a third top-level parent category just to make sure.

So it seems this particular glitch only occurs when:
- Items per Page is set to 1
- Parent row has child row(s) and appears on 1st page

Again, this is not a normal use case (who sets pager for only one item for page anyway?) but it is interesting.

In #13, @nullpixel said:

> Sanic JWT comes with built in endpoints, but they all have set responses, and cannot be overridden as far as I'm aware. Apologies, I should've made the wording clearer.

Currently that is right, there is no way to modify the endpoints without creating a separate [Blueprint](http://sanic.readthedocs.io/en/latest/sanic/blueprints.html) and just inheriting from `sanic_jwt`.

I have not tried this, but something like this should work.

```python
from sanic import Blueprint
from sanic_jwt.blueprint import verify
from json import dumps, loads


custom_bp = Blueprint('custom_bp')

@custom_bp.get('/verify')
async def custom_verify(request, *args, **kwargs):
    response = verify(request, *args, **kwargs)
    raw = loads(response.body)
    raw.update({'foo': 'bar'})
    body = dumps(raw)
    response.body = body
    return response
```

Again, I have not tried this and am not sure if it would work. Also, it would mean your `auth` endpoints might need to be on separate base URL paths. Not ideal.

I definitely do see the need for potentially needing to extend the functionality here. But, the question would be to what extent?

Right now, I am leaning towards implementing a methodology similar to the `payload`. There are built in `handlers` for both building and extending the payload. These can be overwritten using the settings, and are valid as long as they return the necessary key/values. Similarly, we could abstract the auth responses into handlers, and then validate them to make sure the minimum keys are being returned.

Before implementing, some more thought is needed to see exactly what the use cases for this functionality might require.
Along the same lines as the long literals

issue_title: Add feature to support SASL GSSAPI auth options for Kerberos
issue_body:
Feature request to add auth options to support the Enterprise auth SASL methods, which includes GSSAPI (Kerberos).
 
### Expected behavior
There would be a minimal set of server parameters required to configure [Kerberos](https://docs.mongodb.com/manual/tutorial/control-access-to-mongodb-with-kerberos-authentication/).

Mandatory parameters include:
- `--setParameter authenticationMechanisms=GSSAPI`
- The _keytab_ file location (user defined)
- The Service Principle Name as defined in the _keytab_
- Environmental definition `KRB5_KTNAME` for the _keytab_ file location (as above)

Optional parameters include:
- `--setParameter saslHostName=` (FQDN required)
- `--setParameter saslServiceName=` SPN attribute (`mongodb` is the default)
- Environmental definition `KRB5_TRACE` for Kerberos debugging log (recommended)

### Steps to reproduce the actual/current behavior

Using the input parameters above, a typical typical _moongod_ startup syntax would include:

`env KRB5_KTNAME=$dbPath/user.keytab KRB5_TRACE=/$dbPath/krb5.log mongod --config $dbPath/mongod.conf --auth --setParameter authenticationMechanisms=GSSAPI --setParameter saslHostName=$fqdn --setParameter saslServiceName=mongodb`

In Python 3.6.1, I'm experiencing an issue with a `DeprecationWarning` as shown below:

```
lib/python3.6/site-packages/natsort/utils.py:366: DeprecationWarning: generator '_sep_inserter' raised StopIteration
  return lambda split_val, val: tuple(split_val)
```

Code functions and proceeds, however it pops the above DeprecationWarning for every sort.

Appears to be [PEP 479](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0479/) related.  As of Python 3.5, `StopIteration` handling has changed inside generators.

From [PEP 479 section on the Transition Plan](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0479/#transition-plan), it seems like the correct timing as 3.6 is when the non-silent warning was instituted:

>     Python 3.5: Enable new semantics under __future__ import; silent deprecation warning if StopIteration bubbles out of a generator not under __future__ import.
>     Python 3.6: Non-silent deprecation warning.
>     Python 3.7: Enable new semantics everywhere.

The Natsort code itself shows a comment about `StopIteration` inside of the referenced `_sep_inserter` method:
https://github.com/SethMMorton/natsort/blob/master/natsort/utils.py#L233

```
# Get the first element. If StopIteration is raised, that's OK.
```

Seems like we may just need to handle the raised `StopIteration` exception as outlined in PEP 479 and execute inside a `try...except` block to catch `StopIteration`.

issue_title: cookie/session getting lost when session not modified during a request
issue_body:
When using this as a drop-in replacement for flask's default session object, the cookie can unexpectedly get lost if a request is made without a modification to the session. 

When making requests via browser loads, the behavior is not reproducible, probably becuase browsers manage their own cookies across requests such that when an empty cookie is received, the old cookie is used.

The behavior is reproducible if you manage cookies manually on a per-request basis e.g. via `requests` or curl

Example:

### server.py

```python

from flask import Flask, session, request
from flask_kvsession import KVSessionExtension
from simplekv.memory.redisstore import RedisStore
import redis

app = Flask(__name__)
app.secret_key = 'foo'
# toggle for default session
KVSessionExtension(RedisStore(redis.StrictRedis()), app)
@app.route('/')
def index():
    if 'x' not in session:
        session['x'] = 0
    return str(session['x'])
@app.route('/test')
def test():
    session['x'] = session.get('x', 0) + 1
    return str(session['x'])
app.run(debug=True)
```

### client.py

```python
import requests
gcookie = {}
for i in range(10):
    res = requests.get('http://localhost:5000/' + (i%2==0 and 'test' or ''),
                       cookies = gcookie,)
    gcookie = res.cookies
    print res.content
```

Start `server.py` then `client.py`, comparing default sessions with kvsession

expected behavior (default sessions): client will print increasing numbers like 1 1 2 2 3 3 ...

observed behavior (kvsession): client will print 1 1 1 1 1 1...

This seems to happen when `session.modified` is `False`, in `save_session` the response won't contain the session cookie. One can either store the session cookie in the client and continue using it when one isn't returned, or by forcing a `session.modified = True` in e.g. `before_request`.

Would it be a good idea to add some carryover behavior for when the session is not modified, to maintain behavior parity with the default session? Or is it standard to just leave it to the client? Thanks!

issue_title: Saving data with different values for partitioned key crashed
issue_body: Is this supposed to be functional? If, for example I have a Django model that is partitioned over date and one changes the date of that object such that it would cross a partitioning boundary (to go in the next month and therefore the next partition, say), shouldn't the object simply get saved in the new partition? This appears to not be supported and gives me an IntegrityError claiming to violate the check constraint of the date field. Should this be supported or is there some behind the scenes reason this doesn't work or assumption on how this library should be used that this use case violates? Thanks

issue_title: TypeError: _obtain_input_shape() got an unexpected keyword argument 'require_flatten'
issue_body:
I download keras_vggface version4.0. But this error still exists.
I call the function like this: 
vgg_model_conv = VGGFace(include_top=False, input_shape=(224, 224, 3), pooling='avg')

Can you help:)

Hi, @rcmalli . I tried to use your model's predictions usage recently, and it returned me a number result, like "Predicted: 30". What's the number's meaning? And why did the prediction give two totally different number to two different pictures from the same person?
Thanks.

There is no option of configuring plot and iplot in cufflinks.

For example, for removing one of the buttons in plotly.offling, it's possible to use the following:
`py.offline.iplot(data, config={'modeBarButtonsToRemove': ['sendDataToCloud']})`
In cufflinks, however, there is not such option.

issue_title: add appid= override to CLI tools
issue_body:
Folks like @joeyh are writing tools that run `wormhole` as a sub-process. These tools should use a different APPID than the normal text/file/directory-transfer feature that `wormhole send` provides, because there's no meaningful interoperability between a human running `wormhole send MYFILE` and a human running one of those other tools. Distinct APPID values will give them distinct wormhole-code namespaces, so they won't be contending for short channelids.

We need to add a CLI argument to let these subprocess-based applications override the default APPID. This means a new argument to `wormhole.cli.cli.CommonArgs`, and additional code in `wormhole.cli.cmd_send.Sender` (and `cmd_receive.TwistedReceiver`) to use it. `Click`, our argument-parser, makes it easy to use an environment variable too, which might be easier for some front-end tools.

The experimental `wormhole ssh` subcommand does this, but it creates the `Wormhole` object directly (via `wormhole.xfer_util`), rather than invoking `wormhole send` as a subprocess.

issue_title: RevisionManagementError hides other errors when using runserver_plus from django-extensions
issue_body:
When using the runserver_plus server provided by django-extensions (with Werkzeug), some (all?) errors in the application trigger a RevisionManagementError: Request finished with an open revision. All calls to revision.start() should be balanced by a call to revision.end().

E.g. if you try to call a URL with a non-existing view, you get a RevisionManagementError instead, which is quite confusing.

I am not sure whether this is a bug or an incompatibility that is not so easy to avoid. In the latter case, it might be useful to put a hint into the docs.

issue_title: One-to-many relationship with wtforms_alchemy.ModelFieldList does not update children
issue_body:
Below is a form that populates an empty parent object and creates its children. It was necessary to manually invoke a ModelFormField, which was a minor annoyance. It works great. However, when I use the form to do an _update_, only the object is updated -- the children are created fresh.

What is the correct way to propogate an update to the children in this framework? Effectively, I'd like to the two print statements below to print the same thing. I'd be especially grateful if the form would create (delete) children if the formdata had extra (was missing) data for the children.

```
from multidict import MultiDict
from sqlalchemy import Column, ForeignKey, Integer, String
from sqlalchemy.ext.declarative import declarative_base
from sqlalchemy.orm import relationship
from sqlalchemy.orm import sessionmaker
from sqlalchemy import create_engine

from wtforms_alchemy import ModelFieldList, ModelForm, ModelFormField

Base = declarative_base()

class Child(Base):
  __tablename__ = "child"
  id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True)
  name = Column(String)
  parent_id = Column(Integer, ForeignKey("parent.id"))

class Parent(Base):
  __tablename__ = "parent"
  id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True)
  name = Column(String)
  children = relationship(Child)

class ChildForm(ModelForm):
  class Meta:
    model = Child

class ParentForm(ModelForm):
  class Meta:
    model = Parent
  children = ModelFieldList(ModelFormField(ChildForm))  # annoyed!

engine = create_engine("sqlite://")
Base.metadata.create_all(engine)
Session = sessionmaker(bind=engine)
session = Session()

# Form data
formdata = MultiDict({"name": "Foo", "children-0-name": "hotdog"})

# Create initial object
question = Parent()
form = ParentForm(formdata, obj=question)
form.populate_obj(question)
if not form.validate():
  raise RuntimeError(form.errors)
session.add(question)
session.commit()

# prints: "(1, 1)"
print((question.id, question.children[0].id))


# Retrieve object and update with same data
question_get = session.query(Parent).get(question.id)
form = ParentForm(formdata, obj=question_get)
form.populate_obj(question_get)
if not form.validate():
  raise RuntimeError(form.errors)
session.add(question_get)
session.commit()

# prints: "(1, 2)", want it to print the same as above
print((question_get.id, question_get.children[0].id))
```

issue_title: Error response from API when sending underscores
issue_body:
Hi there!
Thanks for your cool API, really easy to use.

I found a problem when trying to send messages that contains underscores.
I investigated a bit and I saw that:
https://api.telegram.org/botMYTOKEN/sendMessage?parse_mode=markdown&chat_id=SOMECHATID&text=Just_making_some_tests
will NOT work.

The solution is quite easy, just escape any _ character like this:
https://api.telegram.org/botMYTOKEN/sendMessage?parse_mode=markdown&chat_id=SOMECHATID&text=Just\_making\_some\_tests
and it will work without issues.

Basically, in my bot I just made `str.replace("_", "\_")` to make it works, but I think it should be fixed in the module.

Antonio

issue_title: cellbg or colspan variable name starting with r fails.
issue_body:
Hi,

When using cellbg or colspan, the variable name given, will be placed by the cellbg and colspan functions:

{% cellbg regular_color %}
{% colspan regular_color %}

The variable name will be inserted into the jinja tag without spaces. If your variable name starts with r it will become: {{regular_color}}

Which will match the following regex in patch_xml since it starts with {{r:

```
# avoid {{r and {%r tags to strip MS xml tags too far
        src_xml = re.sub(r'({{r.*?}}|{%r.*?%})',r'</w:t></w:r><w:r><w:t>\1</w:t></w:r><w:r><w:t>',src_xml,flags=re.DOTALL)
```

Finally this produces an ill-defined XML and creates a nice looking stack trace.

The fix is to change those {{%s}} into {{ %s }} in colspan and cellbg function. I would gladly do it but I'm not sure how to create a pull request. 

Also, as awful as it was, it was very fun tracking down this bug 👍 :)

Looking at the code it appears the columns are loaded in the __new__ method from the class dictionary and dictionaries do not maintain order.

This is a design change. See the original design:  http://sphinx-docfx-yaml.readthedocs.io/en/latest/design.html#yaml-syntax

issue_title: Metrics per view function name instead of URL path?
issue_body:
A new set of metrics for each path makes for a very noisy export, in our case. There could be literally thousands of paths.

Would it be possible to support tracking by the name of the function handling the request, rather than the path? Similar to what [django-prometheus](https://github.com/korfuri/django-prometheus) does.

So that the code:

``` python3
metrics = prometheus_flask_exporter.PrometheusMetrics(app)

@route('/<page_name>')
def process_page(page_name):
    # ...
```

Instead of outputting:

```
flask_http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{le="0.005",method="GET",path="/page1",status="200"} 0.0
flask_http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{le="+Inf",method="GET",path="/page1",status="200"} 1.0
...
flask_http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{le="0.005",method="GET",path="/page2",status="200"} 0.0
flask_http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{le="+Inf",method="GET",path="/page2",status="200"} 1.0
```

Would output:

```
flask_http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{le="0.005",method="GET",function="process_page",status="200"} 0.0
flask_http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{le="+Inf",method="GET",function="process_page",status="200"} 2.0
```

Never done before, what more would be required to do this?

This seems relevant: https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/distributing.html

issue_title: shell_plus: plain: change default for loading Python startup file
issue_body:
Django's `shell` command will load `PYTHONSTARTUP` or `~/.pythonrc.py` by default for the `plain` interpreter.
You can specify `--no-startup` to skip this, but only for the `plain` interpreter: with `-i bpython` or `-i ipython` this has no effect - it gets loaded there through the embedment always.

With `shell_plus` however you need to explicitly enable loading of the startup file (via `--use-pythonrc`) for the `plain` interpreter, which is a bit annoying when you're used to have your `readline` history being configured through `PYTHONSTARTUP`.

It is not an issue once you've installed ipython/bpython etc, since they will also handle the `PYTHONSTARTUP` file with `shell_plus` then.

issue_title: try_passwordless_ssh hangs if ssh key is present, but ssh-agent not running
issue_body:
When an ssh key is present, but no ssh-agent is running (or the key hasn't been added yet), then ssh will ask for the passphrase of the key. The message is in a form like:

    Enter passphrase for key '/home/user/.ssh/id_rsa'

try_passwordless_ssh() will hang in this situation, as this is an unexpected response, and ssh will wait for input indefinitely.

I believe a solution would be to add 'passphrase' to array in the line

    i = p.expect([ssh_newkey, '[Pp]assword:'], timeout=.1)

in tunnel.py. Also, I guess this has to be done in openssh_tunnel()

issue_title: Completion candidates from activated virtual env
issue_body:
Hi,

I'm working on per project virtual env and the completion menu is not populated with objects from the virtual env modules. For exemple, if `pandas` is installed inside a virtual env, and then `import pan<Tab>` (with <Tab> being the key to trigger the completion menu), I get `pattern not found` from my text editor client (neovim).

Then I have tried to look at the pyls code and I came up with the following patch:

```diff
diff --git a/pyls/workspace.py b/pyls/workspace.py
index caf4124..f0e54ae 100644
--- a/pyls/workspace.py
+++ b/pyls/workspace.py
@@ -80,7 +80,13 @@ class Workspace(object):
         """
         files = config.find_parents(self._root_path, path, ['setup.py']) or []
         path = [os.path.dirname(setup_py) for setup_py in files]
-        path.extend(sys.path)
+        try:
+            venv = os.environ['VIRTUAL_ENV']
+        except KeyError:
+            path.extend(sys.path)
+        else:
+            venv_sys_path = jedi.evaluate.sys_path.get_venv_path(venv)
+            path.extend(venv_sys_path)
         return path
```

Now, the `pandas` candidate appears in the completion menu.
I'm not sure if my fix is correct or If I missed something obvious when working with virtual env, so I prefer to keep it as an issue instead of a pull request.

issue_title: Remove jsonrpc and use the published library

issue_body:
Thanks a lot for the server, it's amazing job.

I tried to use it with python3.5 and noticed some issues with mixing `byte` and `str` usage. While it works in python2 (I think mainly because most code and paths are ascii encoded) it raises an exception in 3rd python.

So far I noticed 2 bugs affected by it.

#### Starting a server in `--tcp` mode.

`pyls.server.JSONRPCServer#_content_length` fails at `line.startswith` call, because line is raw bytes and header is an unicode string. I had to modify it to be `line.startswith(b"Content-Length: ")`. And the same applies to next line `_, value = line.split(b"Content-Length: ")`. 

Also sending back response (`pyls.server.JSONRPCServer#_write_message`) fails on write, but it works if encoded - `self.wfile.write(response.encode())`.

But this changes will break the stdin/stdout communication. So perhaps `pyls.language_server.LanguageServer` implementation needs some extra init parameter or attribute to flag if communications are in raw bytes or in unicode string.

#### Open file in VS Code, not directory.

In this case root is not send in initialize call from client, and it breaks the `Workspace`. `self.root` becomes `b''`. And initialize calls succeeds. But all later calls are failing on `pyls.workspace.Workspace#_check_in_workspace`, because it compares `str(doc_path)` with `byte(self.root)`.

The issue here is in `urlparse`. It works both with byte and str, and output depends on input. But for `None` it defaults to bytes

I think there should be some extra support for empty document root. Because in protocol spec it can be null.

```
>>> urlparse('/my/file.txt').path
'/my/file.txt'
>>> urlparse(b'/my/file.txt').path
b'/my/file.txt'
>>> urlparse(None).path
b''
```
This will make it easier to have the documentation synchronized with the code. Also it will make it easier to prepare the release. The manual will be automatically generated by Jenkins.

issue_title: Add more None checks to FieldType coerce methods
issue_body:
Some FieldType classes have a None check, like so;
```python
class IntegerField(BasicField):
    """Integer Coercion Class"""

    def coerce(self, value):
        """Coerces value to int"""

        return int(value) if value is not None else None
```

```python
>>> text_field = TextField()

```
Having None in a DateField will result in an exception.
```python
>>> date_field = DateField()
>>> date_field.coerce(None)
>>> TypeError: Parser must be a string or character stream, not NoneType
```

Should not all FieldType classes support None values safely? I would be happy to add tests and implement None checks for every FieldType. Perhaps by moving the None check up to BasicField.

I'm trying to deploy an OVA using pyvmomi, so I'm using tarfile to extract the ovf file from inside the ova, and then passing that to CreateImportSpec. The ovf contains multi-language comments. When I do this I get 

```
UnicodeEncodeError: 'ascii' codec can't encode character u'\u2122' in position 6825: ordinal not in range(128)
```

However, I'm specifically decoding the file contents as utf-8 (which it is, according to the XML header). Why does this happen? I've pasted the relevant code and full stacktrace below. Python 2.7.11 with pyvmomi 6.0.0.2016.6

```
ovfd = None
ova_path = self.config['nsx-manager']['ova-path']
if path.isfile(ova_path):
    with tarfile.open(ova_path, encoding="utf-8") as tar:
        for tf in tar:
            if tf.name.endswith('.ovf'):
                ovf_filename = tf.name
                logger.debug("Loading %s from %s", ovf_filename, ova_path)
                f = tar.extractfile(tf)
                ovfd = f.read()
                logger.debug(type(ovfd))
                ovfd = ovfd.decode('utf-8')
                logger.debug(type(ovfd))

else:
    raise ValueError("OVA file doesn't exist")
if not ovfd:
    raise ValueError("No OVF found inside OVA archive")

manager = self.vc.si.content.ovfManager

spec_params = vim.OvfManager.CreateImportSpecParams()
import_spec = manager.CreateImportSpec(ovfd,
                                       resourcepool_obj,
                                       datastore_obj,
                                       spec_params)
```

issue_title: Train unit queue uses first building's queue to determine "full"
issue_body:
It seems that train unit queues are not working as expected. If I have three barracks selected and repeatedly  issue the "train marine" command it seems that the queues for each building will increment in a balanced manner until the queue of the first selected barracks is full. At this point the remaining barracks will not add more units to the queue, instead they must wait until the first barracks finishes training a marine.
![unknown](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/998458/36728337-26d7d4f2-1c0c-11e8-81c9-34d58a487314.png)

When I run the command from the command line
    git push -u --all origin
 it fails with
    remote: import failed: User not permitted to commit
and exit code of 1.

But doing the same thing programmatically in GitPython does not show any error at all even though I can see the push was unsuccessful because the changes were not pushed.
This repo appears to no longer be maintained?

1. refine the way to register endpoint by function call: tchannel.register 
2. support decorator to register over class method

issue_title: Unexpected error frames should add the exception message to the error frame
issue_body:
If a handler raises an exception, we currently send a generic error frame back:
https://github.com/uber/tchannel-python/blob/master/tchannel/tornado/dispatch.py#L231

We should add the exception message to the error frame so it provides more information to clients.

It would be great if all languages emitted the inbound.latency stat in the same manner.

This allows us to really compare latencies.

cc @junchaowu

https://sentry.io/biolab/orange3-text-bl/issues/204513206/

issue_title: Facing issue when running multiple scripts on chrome
issue_body:
Hi,
I am running approx 400 scripts on chrome through batch file or RIDE but it's getting crashed during execution.Half of the scripts are getting failed and when opening Report/log file then it's blank. It's happening every time and not able to find the issue.
I am using chrome 30 version and tried it on previous version also.
Please help me to resolve the issue asap.

[Section.parent](https://github.com/G-Node/nixpy/blob/pycore/nixio/pycore/section.py#L36) accessor may point to the wrong object if it is created through a "metadata" hard link.
The method should use `find_sections` to find the correct parent Section object instead.

issue_title: Assumptions of particular keyword argument order don't hold for Python < 3.6
issue_body:
Adding more keyword arguments to `test_snuggs.py:test_arr_lookup` demonstrates the problem

```
    def test_arr_lookup(ones):
        r = snuggs.eval('(read 1)', foo=ones, bar=2.0 * ones, a=3.0 * ones)
>       assert list(r.flatten()) == [1, 1, 1, 1]
E       assert [3.0, 3.0, 3.0, 3.0] == [1, 1, 1, 1]
E         At index 0 diff: 3.0 != 1
E         Use -v to get the full diff
```

This creates problems downstream for Rasterio's rio-calc: https://github.com/mapbox/rasterio/issues/947.

issue_title: NetCDF tempfile handling broken on windows
issue_body:
When using the NCSS client to open data we saved to a tempfile, we get a `RuntimeError` due to "Permission denied". I'm guessing Windows doesn't like someone opening the file a second time.

We could try catching the error and opening a different way, but in that case it's a much bigger pain to ensure the file gets deleted. *sigh* Why can't windows just behave in a unix-like fashion?

This may be obviated once we get the in-memory solution in the netcdf library, but I think it will be awhile after that before it's widely available. Might punt on this until a windows user complains...

issue_title: Destroying the current virtualenv via Pipenv
issue_body:
Hello all, I firstly want to say thank you for the great project. I have used virtualenv + virtualenvwrapper for a year, now I am trying Pipenv as it seems to be moved to pypa project. It feels really comfortable and safe to use.

In comparison with virtualenvwrapper, Pipenv has no command like `rmvirtualenv`. As far as I know, to do this, I check the path for the virtualenv then delete it directly as below.

```bash
$ pipenv --venv
/home/don/.local/share/virtualenvs/esl-S-0IVzfS
$ rmvirtualenv esl-S-0IVzfS  # or
$ rm -rf /home/don/.local/share/virtualenvs/esl-S-0IVzfS
```

The situation that I am concerning is when I delete the project directory and the corresponding virtualenv remains. Of course I may check the unused virtualenv by `lsvirtualenv` and remove it, but I feel that it is not a compact way.

I may be wrong with the usage or the philosophy of Pipenv, are there another way to do this? Or, is the manual removal enough?

Since I searched a bit but I could not find relevant issues, so I leave it here.

##### Describe your environment

1. OS Type: Ubuntu 16.04
1. Python version: 3.5.2
1. Pipenv version: 9.0.1

##### Expected result

Maybe `pipenv destroy`?

I just upgraded `pipenv` to the latest version: `11.1.4`.

After installation was done, I tried to run `pipenv --version`, but it failed.

```batch
C:\windows\system32>pipenv --version
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "c:\python364-32\lib\site-packages\pkg_resources\__init__.py", line 681, in _build_master
    ws.require(__requires__)
  File "c:\python364-32\lib\site-packages\pkg_resources\__init__.py", line 999, in require
    needed = self.resolve(parse_requirements(requirements))
  File "c:\python364-32\lib\site-packages\pkg_resources\__init__.py", line 890, in resolve
    raise VersionConflict(dist, req).with_context(dependent_req)
pkg_resources.ContextualVersionConflict: (psutil 5.4.3 (c:\python364-32\lib\site-packages), Requirement.parse('psutil==5.3.1; sys_platform == "win32"'), {'pew'})
```

##### Describe your environment

1. OS Type - **Windows**
1. Python version: `$ python -V` - **Python 3.6.4**
1. Pipenv version: `$ pipenv --version` - **11.1.4**

##### Expected result


Excepted to received nice output of `pipenv` version.

It happens, of course, no matter which command I choose to to use with `pipenv`.

The same is for just typing `pipenv`

```batch
C:\windows\system32>pipenv
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "c:\python364-32\lib\site-packages\pkg_resources\__init__.py", line 681, in _build_master
    ws.require(__requires__)
  File "c:\python364-32\lib\site-packages\pkg_resources\__init__.py", line 999, in require
    needed = self.resolve(parse_requirements(requirements))
  File "c:\python364-32\lib\site-packages\pkg_resources\__init__.py", line 890, in resolve
    raise VersionConflict(dist, req).with_context(dependent_req)
pkg_resources.ContextualVersionConflict: (psutil 5.4.3 (c:\python364-32\lib\site-packages), Requirement.parse('psutil==5.3.1; sys_platform == "win32"'), {'pew'})
```

##### Actual result

See output below.

##### Steps to replicate

`pip install pipenv --upgrade`

issue_body:
Running update --dry-run shows update to a lower version of the pillow package. 

1. OS Type: Linux (Fedora 26)
1. Python version: 3.6.3
1. Pipenv version: 9.0.1

##### Actual result & Steps to replicate
```
$ mkdir test 
$ cd ptest/
$ pipenv install pillow
[...successful installation...]
$ pipenv update --dry-run --verbose
Checking dependencies…
pillow==4.3.0 is available (5.0.0 installed)!
```
Interestingly `pipenv update` re-installs 5.0.0

issue_title: Excpetion message "No script named ..." in pkg_resources.ResolutionError
issue_body:
We have recently run into this exception when running a script in a package with `PYTHONPATH=.` as part of the unit tests. That causes our package to be imported from the working directory of the repo, which is what we want.

However, `pkg_resources.run_script()` which is used by the generated version of the script does not find the actual version of the script because pkg_resources tries to locate it in the "scripts" directory of the egg metadata directory of the package it has imported. In our `PYTHONPATH=.` case, that is the package in the working directory, which either does not even have a egg metadata directory, or if i create one using "python setup.py egg_info", it has the metadata but not the scripts subdirectory.

With this issue, I do not like to request a solution to this problem, but only a small change that improves the exception message to say where it tried to locate the script. I'll post an according PR soon.

Debugging this to the point to understand where it tried to locate the script has cost me quite some time today. There are tons of occurrences of this issue over the last ten years when you google it, so I'm not alone with this issue (although probably the root causes are different). Having a better exception message helps a lot to understand what's going on, and costs nothing to implement.

BTW, the corresponding issue in our project is pywbem/pywbem#849.

issue_title: Developer guide still refers to bitbucket
issue_body:
I noticed that https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/blob/master/docs/developer-guide.txt still says
```
For posterity, the old `Bitbucket mirror
<https://bitbucket.org/pypa/setuptools>`_ is available.
```
However https://bitbucket.org/pypa/setuptools only contains a README directing the user to this GitHub repo.

Should the "Bitbucket mirror" part be removed from the developer guide?

When trying to create a Jython (installed via Homebrew) env on the latest virtualenv, I get these errors:

```

issue_title: Support running upgrades for multiple sites
Preview image is set to JPG
issue_body:
If the link to a site is posted somewhere (e.g. Slack) and a preview image is loaded for the link, `logo.jpg` will be loaded as defined in:
https://github.com/4teamwork/ftw.simplelayout/blob/master/ftw/simplelayout/opengraph/og_site_root.py#L57

Since we mostly use png-logos, the `logo.jpg` file is in most cases still the default plone logo, which we dont want to show as a preview image if we defined a custom `logo.png`.

I think we should change the line mentioned above to `logo.png`.

issue_title: Double quotes, simple quotes, no quotes...
issue_body:
Is is ok that the behaviour is different whether we wrote this in YAML : 

```
quotes: 
      - "«\xA0"
      - "\xA0»"
      - "‹\xA0"
      - "\xA0›"
```

or this :

```
quotes: 
      - '«\xA0'
      - '\xA0»'
      - '‹\xA0'
      - '\xA0›'
```

On the latter, the backslash is escaped, causing the special character not to be interpreted.

What happens if a write to a broker fails:


stream of conscious


   * we should have super short timeouts so we don't block
   * if there's a failure we should be able to tell the partitioner if we want to
      * move on to next broker.
         * Mark broker as down for the client. Add back in after timeout. (see my cluster code).
      * or immediately raise error.

The 0.10.0 release is scheduled to be cut on March 21, 2016. 

https://cwiki.apache.org/confluence/display/KAFKA/Release+Plan+0.10.0

Here are some changes we should be tracking:

- KIP-32 Add timestamp to message (committed upstream)
    - [KIP](https://cwiki.apache.org/confluence/display/KAFKA/KIP-32+-+Add+timestamps+to+Kafka+message)
    - [Issue](https://issues.apache.org/jira/browse/KAFKA-2511)

-KIP-35 - Retrieving protocol version (in flux)
    - [KIP](https://cwiki.apache.org/confluence/display/KAFKA/KIP-35+-+Retrieving+protocol+version)
   - [Issue](https://issues.apache.org/jira/browse/KAFKA-3304)

issue_title: Docs: For Storm UI, describe relationship between executors, tasks, and workers for Python
issue_body: In trying to explain Storm to a newcomer and how it maps out to Python processes, I myself got tripped up on the differences between slots, executors, tasks, and workers as shown in Storm UI. It would be good for us to have a part of the Quickstart that explains this.

The current HEAD of master has its version in the code set to `'2.0.2'`, though it's several commits beyond the release -- `git describe` currently says `v2.0.2-26-gf4ec238`. The [encoded version](https://github.com/Parsely/streamparse/blob/d9f23d96b280697a6f6775161dd3fa5f4ee0d5ae/streamparse/version.py) should be a qualification against the upcoming release as soon as a release is tagged, as to mitigate confusion where your installation reports a tagged version when you are actually running against a git checkout.

issue_title: Segments should be integers so we can store them in numpy arrays
issue_body:
From Python if you call `tm.getActiveSegments()`, you get a tuple of Segment structs. Instead, this should return a numpy array of integers. That would allow us to pass this list of segments back into C APIs, e.g. into a `mapSegmentsToCells` method.

To do this, we should change the `Connections` to stop having a `Segment` struct. That struct is currently just a wrapper around an integer anyway.

This is a first step toward using a common interface between the two Connections classes (`Connections` and `SparseMatrixConnections`). Eventually I'd like this bindings unit test to pass with either class: https://github.com/numenta/nupic.core/blob/5391ca3d82fe6b5bf915d3570bfc8b1325e5c9ac/bindings/py/tests/connections_test.py

```python

@mvesper is working on this. This report is for reference.

Elasticsearch Queries that include [reserved characters](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/2.3/query-dsl-query-string-query.html#_reserved_characters) are rejected and lead to a 400 response from ES. However in some Elasticsearch installations/versions the error type that Elasticsearch is including in the response is not the same as the one handled by [the error handler](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-records-rest/blob/master/invenio_records_rest/views.py#L68).

On Elasticsearch v2.3.5:
`http :9200/records/_search?q=i%2Fo` >> `error.root_cause.type: "query_parsing_exception"`

On Elasticsearch v2.3.0:
`http :9200/records/_search?q=i%2Fo` >> `error.root_cause.type: "token_mgr_error"`

Possible solutions (besides updating to latest Elasticsearch version) are either extending the dictionary with more error codes, or adding it to the app configuration.

issue_title: Add support for extended an expired token via management command
issue_body:
If a token has expired, it would be useful to be able to extend it through the `create_access_token` management command, rather than just blowing up with a duplicate key database error.

Proposed solution is that if you specify a token that already exists, and an expires value, then this should overwrite the existing expiry.

e.g.

```
# fails if token exists and has expired
python manage.py create_access_token --token foobar
# extends expiry of token by 100 days (from today)
python manage.py create_access_token --token foobar --expires 100
```

issue_title: load_trees() tree-action function is not applied
issue_body:
In `load_trees(filename, tree_action=None)`, the tree action is not applied. The following should work:

```python
def post_action(t):
    t.foo = 'bar'

trees = utils.load_trees(filepath)
for t in trees:
    assert(hasattr(t, 'foo') and t.foo == 'bar')
```

This `tree_action` hook would typically be used to plug in e.g. tree type calculating and setting logic.

Right now, the configuration file for saving credentials is located in the same place as the cartoframes library https://github.com/CartoDB/cartoframes/blob/master/cartoframes/credentials.py#L8-L9.

This will cause permission issues with global installs (`pip install` without virtual env). My suggestion would be to use the os-dependent configuration directory. A good way to do this is to use the [appdirs module](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/appdirs/1.4.3) function, `user_config_dir`. It also has the nice thing that this is per-user instead of per-system. The bad part is yet another dependency.

issue_title: Forcing HTTPBasicAuth in fetch_token results in invalid_request from Google
issue_body:
Using Flask_OAuth2_Login (for example):

```
  def login(self):
    sess = self.session()

    # Get token
    try:
      sess.fetch_token(
        self.token_url,
        code=request.args["code"],
        client_secret=self.client_secret,
      )
```

results in:

```
  File "/lib/python2.7/site-packages/flask_oauth2_login/base.py", line 56, in login
    client_secret=self.client_secret,
  File "/lib/python2.7/site-packages/requests_oauthlib/oauth2_session.py", line 232, in fetch_token
    self._client.parse_request_body_response(r.text, scope=self.scope)
  File "/lib/python2.7/site-packages/oauthlib/oauth2/rfc6749/clients/base.py", line 409, in parse_request_body_response
    self.token = parse_token_response(body, scope=scope)
  File "/lib/python2.7/site-packages/oauthlib/oauth2/rfc6749/parameters.py", line 376, in parse_token_response
    validate_token_parameters(params)
  File "/lib/python2.7/site-packages/oauthlib/oauth2/rfc6749/parameters.py", line 383, in validate_token_parameters
    raise_from_error(params.get('error'), params)
  File "/lib/python2.7/site-packages/oauthlib/oauth2/rfc6749/errors.py", line 271, in raise_from_error
    raise cls(**kwargs)
```

This is due to a change introduced in 0.6.0 in oauth2_session.py/fetch_token:

```
auth = auth or requests.auth.HTTPBasicAuth(username, password)
```

whereas previously auth was allowed to remain empty. Google responds with:

```
{
  "error" : "invalid_request"
}
```

and everything falls down from there. Commenting out the line allows the request to complete normally.

issue_title: num_retries for all requests like googleapiclient
issue_body:
I am confused about the `num_retries` differences between the old `googleapiclient` and the new `google-cloud-python`.

I am able to specify `num_retries` for all API requests with the old `googleapiclient` by either

 1. specifying `num_retries` for all requests
 2. or supplying own `requestBuilder` for the `googleapiclient.discovery.build` which subclasses the original `googleapiclient.http.HttpRequest` and sets `num_retries` for all API requests.

The new `google-cloud-python` allows `num_retries` just for the storage blob upload. Is there other way to specify `num_retries` for other API calls? I think that all API calls (including Datastore, etc.) may fail by HTTP 500 or 429 and then they need to retry the requests, e.g. see https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/exponential-backoff

Also, the `googleapiclient.http._should_retry_response()` seems different than `google.cloud.streaming.http_wrapper.make_api_request()`. E.g. the old one examines the response JSON payload, too.
`core/google/cloud/obselete.py`
https://circleci.com/gh/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/2762

issue_title: Workdirs path length can violate FS constraints with no obvious workarounds for users.
issue_body:
In particular, Aurora hit an issue with thrift codegen as described in https://issues.apache.org/jira/browse/AURORA-1980:

```
Executing tasks in goals: gen -> pyprep -> test
17:13:42 00:01   [gen]
17:13:42 00:01     [thrift-py]
17:13:42 00:01       [cache]    
                   No cached artifacts for 4 targets.
                   Invalidated 4 targets.....
17:13:42 00:01   [pyprep]
17:13:42 00:01     [interpreter]
17:13:46 00:05     [requirements]
17:13:46 00:05       [cache]                                     
                   No cached artifacts for 37 targets.
                   Invalidated 37 targets.
17:14:06 00:25     [sources]
               Waiting for background workers to finish.
17:14:06 00:25   [complete]
               FAILURE
Exception caught: (<type 'exceptions.IOError'>)
 
Exception message: [Errno 36] File name too long: u'/home/user/aurora/.pants.d/build_invalidator/7/pants_backend_python_tasks2_gather_sources_GatherSources/.pants.d.gen.thrift-py.252d64521cf9.api.src.main.thrift.org.apache.aurora.gen._storage.current.api.src.main.thrift.org.apache.aurora.gen._storage.hash'
```

That filename is 255 characters, violating a pretty standard 254 character max filesystem path-length constraint. We should either trap this sort of error centrally and suggest a workaround, or uniformly check for this ourselves and indirect through a fs layer that hashes paths down to a guaranteed length or else, at the very least, patch up for this particular case.

```

issue_title: How to actually get the document contents?
issue_body:
It's been half an hour and I still do not understand how to **simply** read a ".docx" file.
In the **Quickstart** guide you start from explaining how to open an empty document, how to modify it and change fonts and apply styles. While I am convinced that about 95% of python-docx users use it to read ".docx" file and return the contents, nothing else.

So, how do I do that? Why this is not explained in the first place? I am literally doing 
```
doc = Document("test.docx")
print(dir(doc))
```
in order to find an attribute or a method that looks like ``contents``, ``text``, ``get_text``, ``read_data`` whatever... 

Ok, I open up your ``Document`` class source code, I see ``_body``, hmm, nice.
Then I eventually figure out that I need ``Paragraph`` class. 
Finally,
```
for par in doc.paragraphs:
    print par.text
```

Where is a simple method that just does all this and returns you a text?

It looks like the word document is defining shapes using <mc:AlternateContent> tags. In this case it provides multiple "Choice" definitions for the shape. It looks like the "right" choice is to take the first choice which requires "wps". It would have been nice to use the "Fallback" but that doesn't use inline positioning even though the object is in fact inline. Bottom line is the "//w:p/w:r/w:drawing/wp:inline" path is not being found because of the extra tags for the different "choices".

I played a little bit with saving the document as "strict" docx format but that uses a schema derived from purl.oclc.org. That seems to fix the alternative content tags but then it breaks some of the initial document parsing. I suppose being able to handle that would allow for a workaround but that seems like a large undertaking.

issue_title: upgrade pysaml2 to latest release version 4.4.0
issue_body:
I would like to use SATOSA with pysaml2 version 4.4.0 to pick up an enhancement in that package that I requested. The enhancement was to add support for the isMemberOf attribute from the eduMember objectClass. See

https://github.com/rohe/pysaml2/commit/468ac3918f84269d65c628790a6e542b5ee12cd4

That enhancement has been included in pysaml2 version 4.4.0

I'm not sure how to actually create an animated GIF with PIL, so I've resorted to using Image.save like so:

```
from PIL import Image, ImageSequence

f1= Image.new("RGB", (100, 100), "white")
f2= Image.new("RGB", (100, 100), "black")

p= 'test.gif'
f1.save('test.gif', 'GIF', save_all=True, append_images=[f2])

i= Image.open(p)
print('number of saved frames:', len(list(ImageSequence.Iterator(i))))
```

I've confirmed that this does actually call the _save_all function in from GifImagePlugin, and the append_images parameter is also passed there correctly. Even so, the saved GIF only has 1 frame. The code snippet also counts only 1 frame.

Is there a way in `imageio` to get the shape of an image / volume without reading it into memory?

I had a look at the documentation, but couldn't find anything that would do this, as there is 
no shape information in the metadata returned by `Reader.get_meta_data` (at least not for the tiffs I am working with).

This feature would be very useful, e.g. when working with a lot of images and verifying that shapes agree before loading them.

[Version 2017.09.29](https://www.lfd.uci.edu/%7Egohlke/code/tifffile.py.html) brought significant updates for `tifffile.py`, e.g. consolidated access to metadata. At the moment, imageio holds at version 2017.01.12; it would be great if this could be updated.

issue_title: Incorrect tag's assigned when person name and address string contains separator comma's
issue_body:
Without comma we get the proper recipient tags and address tags:
usaddress.parse('JASON BOURNE 90 CLAIRE GULCH CLANCY MT 59634-9528 ')

However, it commas in different spots does not appear to work.
usaddress.parse('JASON BOURNE, 90 CLAIRE GULCH, CLANCY MT 59634-9528 ')
usaddress.parse('JASON, BOURNE, 90 CLAIRE GULCH, CLANCY MT 59634-9528 ')
usaddress.parse('JASON BOURNE, 90 CLAIRE GULCH, CLANCY, MT, 59634-9528')

However, adding c/o, ATT, ATTN, star, FOB etc infront of the above three will produce the correct result (as in the first case above). Example:
usaddress.parse('ATT JASON BOURNE, 90 CLAIRE GULCH, CLANCY, MT, 59634-9528')

issue_title: get_favorite_radio_stations() returns invalid uri on some tunein stations
issue_body:
Hi there,

The get_favorite_radio_stations() method seems to return strange uri in some stations of TuneIn. Maybe it's the station's data wrong or protected?

TO Reproduce:
`import soco
zone = soco.discover().pop()
zone.play_uri('x-rincon-mp3radio://http://live2.rcnmundo.com/lamegacartagena.mp3')`
(This is a radio station from colombia, just as example)

`zone.get_favorite_radio_stations()` returns for this station:
`{u'uri': 'x-sonosapi-stream:s139893?sid=254&flags=8224&sn=0', u'title': 'La Mega (Cartagena) 94.5 (Latino-Pop)'}`

For other stations I get the real url:
`{u'uri': 'x-rincon-mp3radio://http://www.radioswissjazz.ch/live/aacp.m3u', u'title': 'Radio Swiss Jazz'}`

The SoCo docs state
```
These properties are not generally cached and may obtain information over
the network, so may take longer than expected to set or return a value.
It may be a good idea for you to cache the value in your own code.
```
However, the library does have a caching logic, which is only used for GetZoneGroupState (see cache.py and services.py > send_command). Since caching is generally a good idea, it would be great if we could offer the user an option to cache properties as well.

There are two options for how to proceed:

- Expand core.py to offer caching settings, using the existent service-level caching logic in services.py 
  - Pro: No code duplication
  - Con: Bloats up core.py further

- Build a SoCo subclass that handles caching, expanding the service-level caching logic in services.py 
  - Pro: Clean user interface
  - Pro: No code duplication
  - Con: Hard to implement because the subclass doesn't do the calls to services.py
  - Con: Dependency problems

- Build a SoCo subclass that handles caching, and stores the high-level return values of the SoCo methods
  - Pro: Clean user interface
  - Con: Code duplication

Which would you vote for?

issue_title: License instances are overwriting each other when being merged into main DB
issue_body:
## Summary

Saw a few cases of the wrong license showing under content. Turns out we use an auto-incrementing ID on License in the Kolibri models, and only export the licenses used in a channel, so the pk's don't always correspond to a particular license. Then, when we merge them into the joint DB, they overwrite each other (or don't, but in any case, many license FK's end up pointing to the wrong license.

In fixing this, we'll need to think holistically across Studio models, the export process, and the import process, and take into account Special Permissions, which come with custom text (so there's more than one Special Permissions license, in effect).

## How to reproduce

1. Import 66cef05505fa550b970e69c3623e82ba and 8b28761bac075deeb66adc6c80ef119c
1. Go to content pages
1. Depending on which channel was loaded first, content from one channel will show the wrong license.

## Screenshots

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/618416/31102718-3c8df5ea-a788-11e7-925a-5d4971d6898d.png)

## Real-life consequences

Boatloads of legal trouble for everyone! :)

issue_title: Enrolling a large number (~10) of learners into a class causes an error
issue_body:
Originally discussed in in #2073.

Batch enrolling Learners into a class creates 1 request per Learner. When enrolling multiple learners, this seems to cause a race condition where the database can be locked performing one transaction while another one is being attempted.

One proposed solution is to allow the relevant endpoint to accept many learners to add to a class, so only one request is made for the whole batch.

issue_title: Add Email Sending Functionality to Deis
issue_body:
It would be really useful if deis could send emails. You could do things like reset passwords, setup invite only invitation, email admins when a component or apps fails or errors appear in the log. It could also notify admins when new users or apps are created.

This would also be simple to setup. It would entail adding a STMP server component in a docker container, [such as this postfix container here](https://registry.hub.docker.com/u/linuxkonsult/postfix/), the deis controller being wired up to use it, and possibly having the user set up a [SPF policy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sender_Policy_Framework) to authorize deis to email using their deis domain.

issue_title: Consider enumerating etcd peers instead of using a discovery URL
issue_body:
The etcd discovery service is meant only to bootstrap a new cluster, and there's no guarantee that the endpoint will work indefinitely. This can prevent users from adding new hosts to an existing CoreOS cluster. We should switch from a discovery service to manually specifying the cluster's peers.

To do this, we'll need to edit [user-data.example](https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/master/contrib/coreos/user-data.example) to replace the discovery URL with a list of etcd peers. We'll also need to specify IP addresses on each cloud provider so the hosts come up with a known IP address. We should be able to do this on AWS by disabling DHCP, but other providers may be more difficult.

So, I've been playing around with web3 in an attempt to setup a py.test plugin leveraging web3 and eth-tester. A fairly common scenario in my testing is to check that a method call fails when attempted. I have a pytest fixture `assert_tx_failed` that takes a snapshot, attempts the call, records whether it raises a specified exception (default is `EthereumTester.exceptions.TransactionFailed`, but overridable), and reverts to the snapshot, returning whether the recorded call was successful or not. I usually use it like this:
```python
def test_foo(assert_tx_failed):
    c = contract_fixture('Foo')
    assert_tx_failed(c.foo()) # Makes a call to 'foo()' in 'Foo' contract
```
I think it would be nicer if there was a modifier in there that did this (perhaps under the umbrella of the `ImplicitContract` class I added, renamed to something like `TestContract`). It would then look like this:
```python
def test_foo():
    c = contract_fixture('Foo')
    assert c.foo().fails() # returns True only if 'TransactionFailed' occurs
    assert c.foo().fails(exception=FooException) # returns True only if that exception occurs
    # NOTE: IIRC there is only the one possible exception from Ethereum
```
Conceptually, this would be a lot more straightforward in a testing scenario, and reduces a commonly used test fixture to be built-in (reducing overall codebase management).


Side note: building a py-test plugin seems like it might be fairly easy leveraging web3 and eth-tester with some custom fixture-generation scheme leveraging the standard abi/bin compiler output (stored in JSON) to generate contract fixtures for specific contracts in your directory
I wonder why you did not elect to create aggregated wheels instead which is a scenario that has been always support by wheel? (eg rewheeling several wheels in one single wheel?)

issue_title: Exception logged in Windows from select() due to Rejected event
issue_body:
The following trivial script results in a `log.exception()` message in Windows, which doesn't seem like the expected behavior.

```
import lomond

ws = lomond.WebSocket('ws://www.google.com')
events = list(ws.connect())
print(events)
```

Output:
```
[Connecting(url='ws://www.google.com'), Connected(url='ws://www.google.com'), Rejected(<response HTTP/1.1 400 Bad Request>, 'Websocket upgrade failed (code=400)'), Disconnected('error; file descriptor cannot be a negative integer (-1)', graceful=False)]
error in websocket loop
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "C:\Projects\venv\websocket_client_playground\lib\site-packages\lomond\session.py", line 384, in run
    readable = selector.wait_readable(poll)
  File "C:\Projects\venv\websocket_client_playground\lib\site-packages\lomond\selectors.py", line 32, in wait_readable
    select.select([self._socket.fileno()], [], [], timeout)
ValueError: file descriptor cannot be a negative integer (-1)
```

Environment:
* Python 3.5.3
* lomond 0.2.1
* Windows 10 Pro 1709 16299.309

When a `HandshakeError` is encountered in `WebSocket.feed()`, leading to a `Rejected` event, the socket is ultimately closed.  The issue is with the call to `select()` in `SelectSelector.wait_readable()` which happens after this, as `self._socket.fileno()` will return -1.

A trivial solution is to wrap the code in `wait_readable()` in a blanket try/except and return `True` in the failure case.  This will allow normal processing to proceed in `WebsocketSession.run()` and the session will close out gracefully.

issue_title: Feature: Add a hook for post_report phase
issue_body:
Once an entire regression is finished, its very practical if the user could specify an action which can be done after that, for example sending an automated mail/sms to the user. This is particularly useful in case of long regressions which are submitted to a batch queue.
In the current implementation, everything that comes after the ui.main() is not executed, since the main function calls the end of the script automatically.
Therefore, a hook or an alternative could be used to specify any action that would take place after the summary/report generation is finished.

issue_title: InstallAzureCli installer doesn't work in non-interactive mode
issue_body:
The `InstallAzureCli` installer doesn't work except when the user can provide terminal input via a Mac/Linux tty device.    Even if you type `yes '' | bash InstallAzureCli` after downloading the script, it doesn't work because it insists on the tty device.

The use of a tty is quite unnecessary given there are no passwords or input line editing features involved here.

This is important for use with Ansible, Chef, Puppet, Docker (building your own image), etc. My application is to ensure that a server used to run Azure CLI commands can be re-provisioned entirely by Ansible.

I'm aware of the bundled installer (#3376), but that is now quite out of date (2.0.10 vs current 2.0.20) and I need a recent version of azure-cli to fix a concurrency bug with `clouds.config`.  Same applies to APT packages.

Please can you provide a command line option such as `bash InstallAzureCli --non-interactive` so that this works?  Or just enable `yes '' | bash InstallAzureCli` by reading from standard input instead of a tty?

Or alternatively, build the bundled installer for every Azure CLI release.

Many other tools such as `dpkg` provide such options.   I'm not aware of a workaround, except perhaps for running the installer under `expect` which is quite a hassle for a CLI tool.

---

### Environment summary

Install Method / OS version / Shell Type

```
InstallAzureCli installer, under Ansible, on macOS 10.11 and Ubuntu 16.04.

Azure CLI version: azure-cli (2.0.20) - and earlier versions

Using bash (or any shell)

```

issue_title: [DNS] 'az network dns zone delete' and 'az network dns record-set delete' should not support '--if-none-match'
issue_body:
The if-none-match HTTP header is only applicable on the Azure DNS REST API when creating a zone.

Therefore, `az network dns zone delete` should not support `--if-none-match`.  (It makes no sense...it means 'delete this, but only if it doesn't exist already' !!)

The same applies to `az network dns record-set delete`.

Please remove this parameter.

issue_title: Please enter password for encrypted keyring
issue_body:
During the custom Linux build agent deployment for Visual Studio Team Services (Ubuntu Server 18.04-LTS) I noticed that there is an issue with something I did not noticed previously on Ubuntu Server and I'm not noticing on my Mac OS X. When I'm trying to run Python (3.6) script, which is using Azure SDK, I'm receiving interactive prompt "Please enter password for encrypted keyring". It's not a problem to create keyring and provide a password when running script interactively but when we are talking about automation or in a context of VSTS it is not acceptable to have any interactive steps in the process. I have started some investigation on that: https://lnx.azurewebsites.net/please-enter-password-for-encrypted-keyring-when-running-python-script-on-ubuntu/

The root cause is File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/dist-packages/msrestazure/azure_active_directory.py", line 207, in _default_token_cache

where Ubuntu 18.04 (and 17.10), when installing python3-pip package is, by default, also installing python3-wheel, python3-keyring and python3-keyrings.alt.

SO, by default, msrestazure is trying to use system keyring, prompting for password. It's hard to automate anythong, having interactive prompt.

If there is a keyring-enabled solution, please provide it in the documentation. If not, maybe you should consider using PlaintextKeyring() by default?

issue_title: Don't apply abbreviation expansions for sources in non en-US locale
issue_body:
In Italy, most roads are named `Via xyz` where [*via*](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/via#Italian) means *road* or *street* in English. The current [abbreviation expansion code](https://github.com/openaddresses/machine/blob/master/openaddr/expand.py#L214) transforms each of these to *Viaduct*. See [Firenze](https://github.com/openaddresses/openaddresses/blob/master/sources/it/52/firenze.json) [for example](http://s3.amazonaws.com/data.openaddresses.io/runs/57159/it/52/firenze.zip).

As the expansion code is only made for [US English](https://github.com/openaddresses/machine/blob/master/openaddr/expand.py#L2) abbreviations, it doesn't make sense to apply it to anything other than sources in the US.

I find the following don't really have any good use cases (and don't come up in normal day-to-day) and are undocumented beyond `--help`.  **I'm proposing removing these options**:

## `pre-commit run --no-stash`

This disables the auto-stashing of files when running -- though this is already the case for `pre-commit run --all-files` and `pre-commit run --files ...`.

The behaviour of `--no-stash` (without using `--no-stash`) can be achieved via `git diff --name-only | xargs pre-commit run --files`

It was added [along with the avoiding behaviour](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit/pull/80) in the same pull request.  I want to say this was my first idea for "fixing" the problem and then I forgot to undo it.

## `pre-commit run --allow-unstaged-config`

This (unfortunately) collides with `pre-commit run --all` (prefix match of `pre-commit run --all-files) so I've wanted to get rid of it anyway.

This allows one to run with an unstaged configuration, which then (in most cases) gets the changes swiped out from under you and causes confusing situations where the hooks that are run aren't what was on disk at the time of running.  The warning that's printed when doing this also explains this.

This was [originally my idea](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit/issues/157#issuecomment-99080756) but now I think we can just do without the option at all -- requiring the pre-commit configuration to be staged when running pre-commit.

issue_title: Cached aiohttp.web.Response objects cause requests to hang
issue_body:
## Long story short

<!-- Please describe your problem and why the fix is important. -->
If you use a cached `aiohttp.web.Response` in a middleware function it will cause any subsequent requests to hang.

## Expected behaviour

<!-- What is the behaviour you expect? -->
After thinking for a bit, I suggest that we either:

* Improve documentation, by showing an example of what to do if you wish to cache the response body, or
* Make the response object re-usable between requests. It can still remain unchangeable after `prepare()` has been called, preserving its semantics. However, this is probably not feasible with the current architecture.

## Actual behaviour

<!-- What's actually happening? -->
As far as I understand, `aiohttp.web.Response` is a _Finite State Machine_. Once a `Response`
object is dispatched by the server, it must be emptying some internal buffer. This renders it unusable for subsequent dispatches.

The current solution for avoiding this behaviour seems to be having the middleware generate a new `Response` object, everytime it is called.

## Steps to reproduce

<!-- Please describe steps to reproduce the issue.
     If you have a script that does that please include it here within
     markdown code markup -->
Here is a simple application that reproduces the behaviour:

```python
from aiohttp import web


def my_middleware_factory():

    # this could have been passed in as a default arg, or exist as a global
    state = web.Response(status=200, text='this is cached')

    @web.middleware
    async def middleware(request, handler):
        res = await handler(request)
        return state

    return middleware


async def handler(request):
    return web.Response(status=200, text='Hello World!')


def main():
    app = web.Application()
    app.router.add_get('/', handler)
    app.middlewares.append(my_middleware_factory())

    web.run_app(app)


if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()

```

* When you hit `http://localhost:8080` twice
* Then the second request should hang your http client

## Your environment

<!-- Describe the environment you have that lead to your issue.
     This includes aiohttp version, OS, proxy server and other bits that
     are related to your case. 
     
     IMPORTANT: aiohttp is both server framework and client library.
     For getting rid of confusing please put 'server', 'client' or 'both'
     word here.
     -->
Python 3.6
aiohttp=3.2.1
Ubuntu 16.04
'server'
w/ progress callbacks somehow

issue_title: The asterisk for "busy cell" is ambiguous
issue_body:
When a cell is displayed as "busy" with an asterisk, it can mean one of several things:

1. The cell is currently executing.
2. An "execute" command was sent to the cell, but another cell is currently executing. The cell will execute when its turn comes.
3. The kernel was interrupted / restarted while the cell was executing, it is currently not executing.

(There might be another scenario that I'm missing here.)
This ambiguity makes it hard to handle notebooks with cells that take some time to execute and are sometimes queued or interrupted. It's hard to understand the current execution status. Often I find myself returning to a notebook and not remembering if the cell is still executing or not.

It would be great to have some way to distinguish the above cases, maybe by using a different symbol for each one instead of using an asterisk for all of them.

issue_title: Add more specific metadata to request headers
issue_body:
For the purposes of tracking usage adoption, it would be nice to include some library and version information in the HTTP requests. 

I believe the `requests` library under the hood of `searchtweets` sends something similar to `'User-Agent': 'python-requests/1.2.0'` in its requests. Perhaps we could modify the headers like (roughly): 
```python
version = searchtweets.__version__
useragent = 'search-tweets-python/{}'.format(version)
headers = {'User-Agent': useragent}
```
I'm not sure if it would be best set in the [`request()` method](https://github.com/twitterdev/search-tweets-python/blob/master/searchtweets/result_stream.py#L120) or in [the `make_session()` method](https://github.com/twitterdev/search-tweets-python/blob/master/searchtweets/result_stream.py#L30). The `requests` docs mention doing it in both places ([former](http://docs.python-requests.org/en/latest/user/quickstart/#custom-headers), [latter](http://docs.python-requests.org/en/latest/user/advanced/#prepared-requests)).

@binaryaaron @fionapigott @jeffakolb are there other metadata things that would be useful in the headers?

issue_title: Pause/resume runs into deadlock when rebalancing
issue_body:
Description
===========
I'm trying to integrate the pause/resume functionality in our kafka service. By moving the actual message processing to a separate worker process, I keep the main thread calling the poll method to quickly react to rebalances by interrupting the worker process.

While developing this it appears to me that it's possible to get the consumer is some sort of paused state. In that case the poll method doesn't return any new messages. See attached example.

What is the appropriate way of using pause and resume in a thread-safe way?

How to reproduce
================
I run the following dummy program twice, next to each other. I interrupt and restart one of the two processes to invoke rebalances. After a couple of times, one of the two processes stops consuming new messages from Kafka.

```
import confluent_kafka
from confluent_kafka import TopicPartition
import time

class TestService(object):
    def __init__(self):
        self.consumer = confluent_kafka.Consumer(
            **{
                'bootstrap.servers': "localhost:29092",
                'group.id': "cg-test-02",
                'api.version.request': True,
                'fetch.wait.max.ms': 1000,
                'fetch.min.bytes': 1,
                'fetch.message.max.bytes': 10*1024*1024,
                'queued.min.messages': 1,
                'queued.max.messages.kbytes': 55000,
                'enable.auto.commit': 'false',
                'receive.message.max.bytes': 900000000,
                'session.timeout.ms': 10000,
                'heartbeat.interval.ms': 1000,
                'socket.timeout.ms': 20000,
                'default.topic.config': {
                    'auto.offset.reset': 'earliest'
                },
            }
        )

        self.partitions = []
        self.paused = False
        self.shutting_down = False

    def on_assign(self, consumer, partitions):
        print partitions
        consumer.assign(partitions)
        self.partitions = partitions
        self.consumer.resume(self.partitions)

    def on_revoke(self, consumer, partitions):
        self.partitions = []
        consumer.unassign()

    def run(self):
        self.consumer.subscribe(topics=["topic-a"], on_revoke=self.on_revoke, on_assign=self.on_assign)

        while True:
            self.consumer.resume(self.partitions)

            msg = self.consumer.poll(timeout=0.2)
            if msg is not None:
                print(str(msg.partition()) + ": " + str(msg.offset()) + "")
                time.sleep(0.1)
                self.commit(msg.topic(), msg.partition(), msg.offset())
            else:
                print "None"

            self.consumer.pause(self.partitions)

    def commit(self, topic, partition, offset):
        topic_partition_to_commit = TopicPartition(topic=topic, partition=partition, offset=offset + 1)
        self.consumer.commit(offsets=[topic_partition_to_commit], async=True)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    service = TestService()
    service.run()
```


Checklist
=========
Please provide the following information:

 - [x] confluent-kafka-python and librdkafka version (`('0.11.4', 721920)` and `('0.11.4', 722175)`):
 - [x] Apache Kafka broker version: 1.0.0
 - [x] Client configuration: `{...}`
 - [x] Operating system: MacOS 10.13.3
 - [ ] Provide client logs (with `'debug': '..'` as necessary)
 - [ ] Provide broker log excerpts
 - [ ] Critical issue

As I interpret the documentation at http://docs.confluent.io/3.1.1/clients/confluent-kafka-python/ , the parameter I give Consumer.poll() tells it to block for that amount of time (seconds? The document does not specify any units, it just says "maximum time to wait") if nothing interesting happens before the timeout. So I would imagine that when I call poll(2.0), if there was a message to receive, I would get that message immediately, but if not, I would block for 2 seconds. But it looks like I always return immediately, no matter what I give as a timeout to poll(), even when there are no messages or errors returned. Am I misinterpreting something?

I tried to understand the underlying rd_kafka_q_pop_serve(), but I'm not confident that I understand it. I wonder if my python callbacks (on_commit, on_assign, error_cb, stats_cb) should return a value, 0 or 1. Right now I just return, no value at all. The documentation does not mention any return value from the callbacks.

Thanks.
