Two different processes are making requests at the same time.

```
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/descarteslabs/auth/__init__.py", line 101, in token
    self._get_token()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/descarteslabs/auth/__init__.py", line 171, in _get_token
    os.makedirs(path)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/os.py", line 157, in makedirs
    mkdir(name, mode)
OSError: [Errno 17] File exists: '/root/.descarteslabs'`
```

I'm looking at the available imagery sources:

```python
from pprint import pprint
import itertools

import descarteslabs as dl

sources = dl.metadata.sources()
groups = {}
for group, items in itertools.groupby(sources, key=lambda v: v['const_id']):
    groups[group] = list(items)
pprint(groups)
```

This generates the following sources:

```
{'CDL': [{'const_id': 'CDL', 'sat_id': 'CDL', 'value': 140}],
 'L5': [{'const_id': 'L5', 'sat_id': 'LANDSAT_4', 'value': 119}],
 'L7': [{'const_id': 'L7', 'sat_id': 'LANDSAT_7', 'value': 112}],
 'L8': [{'const_id': 'L8', 'sat_id': 'LANDSAT_8', 'value': 89}],
 'L8SR': [{'const_id': 'L8SR', 'sat_id': 'L8SR', 'value': 142}],
 'NAIP': [{'const_id': 'NAIP', 'sat_id': 'NAIP', 'value': 121}],
 'S1A': [{'const_id': 'S1A', 'sat_id': 'SENTINEL-1A', 'value': 108}],
 'S1B': [{'const_id': 'S1B', 'sat_id': 'SENTINEL-1B', 'value': 107}],
 'S2A': [{'const_id': 'S2A', 'sat_id': 'S2A', 'value': 111}],
 'S3A': [{'const_id': 'S3A', 'sat_id': 'S3A', 'value': 110}],
 'modis09': [{'const_id': 'modis09', 'sat_id': 'Terra', 'value': 3},
             {'const_id': 'modis09', 'sat_id': 'Aqua', 'value': 4}],
 'srtm': [{'const_id': 'srtm', 'sat_id': 'srtm', 'value': 120}]}
```

However, there are some inconsistencies here:

* There's no `L4`, as shown in the documentation [here](http://descartes-labs-python.readthedocs.io/en/latest/getting_started.html#Searching-for-available-imagery). It looks like the sources have been updated since the documentation was written.
* `L5`'s description is `LANDSAT_4`. Is this a typo?
* What exactly is `L8SR`, and what makes it different from `L8`?
* What is `srtm`?

I guess the broader issue is that `dl.metadata.sources()` doesn't adequately describe each source, and there's no source-specific documentation on http://descartes-labs-python.readthedocs.io/en/latest/ either. I might propose a few things:

* Add another field, perhaps `description`, that includes a human-readable description of the source.
* You could provide URL fields. `wiki_url`, for example, could provide a link to the Wikipedia article that describes the data source (e.g., https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Landsat_8). `official_url` might link to the *official* website of the source (e.g., https://landsat.usgs.gov/landsat-8).
* Add a section on http://descartes-labs-python.readthedocs.io/en/latest/ that describes each data source you guys offer. Each data source could have a description of available bands, spatial resolution, temporal resolution, years in operation, limitations, example use cases, etc.

Now that we have our first release we need to make a decision regarding how to handle the git tree. This was discussed a bit in Sandia-Labs/PVLIB_Python/issues/33, and @uvchik brought it up in #15. 

I always look first to copy IPython and/or pandas. The [ipython system](https://github.com/ipython/ipython/wiki/Dev%3A-GitHub-workflow) is roughly:

* ``master`` should be the next major release. Now that ``0.1`` is on pypi, this would mean that the ``master`` version should be updated to something like ``0.2.0dev``. See [``release.py``](https://github.com/ipython/ipython/blob/master/IPython/core/release.py)
* Branches should be created for any maintenance releases. So, if we're going to bother with a ``0.1.1`` release then it should be developed in a ``0.1.x`` branch.

So far as I can tell, pandas just keeps bumping the version number by 0.0.1 or 0.1 according to the scope of whatever has been merged. Maybe they let some bigger PRs rot for awhile if they're trying to focus on a maintenance release.

In principle I like the IPython system, but in practice, I think that we should go with the pandas system at this early stage. The IPython system probably makes more sense if/when we get to 1.0 and 2.0 releases. However, I don't care too much about this (we just need a decision so that we can update the version file), and I am especially willing to go with the IPython system if somebody else volunteers to be the main administrator of the maintenance releases and development.

Whatever we decide on should be added to the [Development wiki](https://github.com/pvlib/pvlib-python/wiki/Development-information).

Dilated convolution is currently implemented as a convolution backward pass because Theano did not have any other support for this. For performance reasons (avoiding a dimshuffle -> gpu_contiguous), the weights are expected in `(in_channels, out_channels, rows, columns)` layout. In the meantime, Theano has been extended to support dilated convolution as a forward pass operation via Caffe's gemm-based convolution, and it's possible cuDNN will follow suit.

Before releasing Lasagne 0.2, I think we should:
* [ ] change the implementation to use the standard layout for `W`: `(out_channels, in_channels, rows, columns)`
* [ ] use the forward pass interface if Theano supports it (we still want to be compatible to a PyPI-hosted Theano release in Lasagne 0.2, so currently we cannot rely on that interface being present)
* [ ] compare the performance of the backward pass trick to the Caffe implementation
* [ ] if the backward pass trick is faster (I actually doubt that), update Theano to use it whenever possible

issue_title: dropout layers leak float64 into graph
issue_body:
When using a dropout layer, the output upcasts to float64, causing every layer after it to be upcast to float64. The problem seems to be caused by the way numpy treats operations that upcast integers. For examples:

x = 1 - numpy.float32(0.5)

causes x to have type numpy.float64

This happens to me on numpy version 1.9.0, but I think it happens on other versions as well.

So, here is a code fix:

Line 73 of noise.py - which reads:
retain_prob = 1 - self.p

should be changed to something like this:
if theano.config.floatX == 'float64' :
                retain_prob = np.float64(1 - self.p)
else :
                retain_prob = np.float32(1 - self.p)

Parameters to NWBAttributeSpec should be:

@papajohn @SamLau95 
Hi! I'm the GSI for the smart cities connector course. I talked to Sam about the mapping tool this morning. I was wondering if we could add a method to overlay map features over an existing map. Basically the issue I ran into is that after I create a map I may want to add markers or labels to the map, and so far there's not an easy way to do that:

I wrote the following method that seems to do the trick. Could this be added to the Map tool?
The only other issue with the function is that in my implementation I edit the Map._folium_map directly. It might be nice if we copy and return a new Map object instead of modifying the existing Map in place. I couldn't figure out the best way to do this. If you have time, it would be great if you could take a look. Let me know if you have any questions.

Best,
Maddie

def overlay(feature, ds_map, color='Blue', opacity=.6):
    '''
    feature can be a table of map features, a list of map features,
    a Map, or a Region. The features will be drawn on the existing 
    Map (ds_map). 
    '''
        
    if type(feature) == Table:
        # if table of features e.g. Table.from_records(taz_map.features)
        if 'feature' in feature:
            feature = feature['feature']
            
        # if marker table e.g. table with columns: latitudes,longitudes,popup,color,radius
        else:
            feature = Circle.map_table(feature)
            
    if type(feature) in [list, np.ndarray]:
        for f in feature:
            f._attrs['fill_color'] = color
            f._attrs['fill_opacity'] = opacity
            f.draw_on(ds_map._folium_map)
    
    elif type(feature) == Map:
        for i in range(len(feature._features)):
            f = feature._features[i]
            f._attrs['fill_color'] = color
            f._attrs['fill_opacity'] = opacity
            f.draw_on(ds_map._folium_map)
    elif type(feature) == Region:
        feature._attrs['fill_color'] = color
        feature._attrs['fill_opacity'] = opacity
        feature.draw_on(ds_map._folium_map)
    return ds_map

From #54 it's clear that maintaining compatibility with ancient versions of Django provides a burden and demotivates people to submit/fix PR's/tests.

I suggest dropping support for Django versions < 1.8, and following Django's support scheme. People stuck on old versions of Django can use older django-webtest versions that are compatible.

This would require shim code to be cleaned up, build config to be updated and a proper document that states wich version of django-webtest supports which Django versions.

issue_title: event.headers returns array of bytearray since 0.8.0
issue_body: In the process of upgrading wsproto to h11 0.8.0 I noticed that the _normed_header_dict method https://github.com/python-hyper/wsproto/blob/cbb93a34808cf4894914e91df81e99f957f2753d/wsproto/connection.py#L49 (which receives headers directly from h11 without any transformations as far as I'm aware) receives an array of strings when using h11 0.7.0 and an array of bytearrays when using h11 0.8.0 which breaks wsproto when using h11 0.8.0.

Hey, just FYI: hyperlink encodes query to UTF-8 before escaping (`_encode_query_part` function); this is incorrect, as query part should be encoded to page encoding. See https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#url-query-string.

issue_title: Taking action limits into account in PPO/TRPO/ACKTR.
issue_body:
This is more of a question than an issue. I noticed that in the implementation of the above-mentioned algorithms, action limits are not taken into account. Environments handle this clipping internally, so no errors will appear, but this brings us to situations where the algorithm in it's training batch has inputs that were not necessarily applied within the environment.

For example, let's say the upper limit for an input is 1 and the applied input (given from the algorithm) is 5. What the environment will experience is input 1, due to it's internal clipping, but the algorithm will in it's training batch have data with action equal to 5. 

Intuitively it makes sense that the algorithm will learn how to deal with this, but I am wondering if using the information of exactly what action is applied would be beneficial? Additionally, we can think of applying the action clipping even before adding noise (since noise doesn't really do anything if the mean is already out of limits). For example, DDPG handles this nicely with tanh outputs before applying the noise, and with clipping applied afterwards.

issue_title: Best way to work with relay
Scalar types not valid in graphql-core
issue_body:
It can be handy to drop down to graphql-core types for low level things. However currently Graphene's scalar types don't pass graphql-core's type checking. This makes it hard to mix and match. Since Graphene is build on graphql-core, it seems like this should be doable.

Example of failing type check.
https://github.com/graphql-python/graphql-core/blob/master/graphql/core/type/definition.py#L7

I've run into a bug with the AsyncIO Executor when trying to run a query from an application where the event is running. 

I think it's because the executor's implemention of `wait_until_complete` uses `run_until_complete` which starts up the event loop and waits. Since the event loop is already running, it threw an exception when I tried to start it a second time.

issue_title: [nrf52840] cc310 is not initialized in some cases
issue_body:
CC310 is supposed to be initialized during platform init, but I noticed an issue that CC310 will not be initialized if OT dtls related features are not enabled (commissioning, joiner, border agent).

The way to reproduce:

1. Build with: ```make -f examples/Makefile-nrf52840 USB=1```
[cc310_mbedtls.c](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/master/third_party/NordicSemiconductor/libraries/crypto/cc310_mbedtls.c) is **not** built in, the blank [nrf5CryptoInit()](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/master/examples/platforms/nrf52840/misc.c#L45) is used instead (There seems no issue to do AES using CC310 without initializing it).

2. Build with: ```make -f examples/Makefile-nrf52840 COMMISSIONER=1 USB=1```
[cc310_mbedtls.c](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/master/third_party/NordicSemiconductor/libraries/crypto/cc310_mbedtls.c) is built in, and platform init will call into [nrf5CryptoInit()](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/master/third_party/NordicSemiconductor/libraries/crypto/cc310_mbedtls.c#L44) to initialize CC310.

@rlubos Is it as expected?

issue_title: Use Python GAPIC package name to determine Python gRPC package name
issue_body:
### What

The Python GAPIC package name is currently configured in the GAPIC config. The Python gRPC package name is determined by combining the prefix `google.cloud.grpc` with the proto package name. For consistency, it would be good to use the same package name structure for both GAPIC and gRPC; that is, any GAPIC package `{prefix}.gapic.{suffix}` will depend on the gRPC package `{prefix}.grpc.{suffix}`.

### Why

This avoids the verbosity of some proto package names while ensuring a consistent naming strategy across Python packages. See @bjwatson's comment in https://github.com/googleapis/api-client-staging/pull/154

### Concerns

The simplest implementation requires `artman` to read the GAPIC config to obtain the GAPIC package name and pass it to the gRPC package renaming task. I'm wary about introducing a dependency on the GAPIC config for a gRPC pipeline, since none currently exists, and such a dependency is counterintuitive (GAPIC should depend on gRPC, not the other way around).

An alternative is to move package name configuration to the artman config. This has the drawback that the package name must still be propagated to the GAPIC config (so it's duplicated across configs) and introduces the possibility of inconsistency if the GAPIC config is manually edited, but doesn't introduce any new dependencies to the gRPC pipeline. 

cc: @bjwatson, @lukesneeringer

issue_title: certbot certificates should explain why it thinks configurations are invalid
issue_body:
If you're having trouble using Certbot and aren't sure you've found a bug or
request for a new feature, please first try asking for help at
https://community.letsencrypt.org/. There is a much larger community there of
people familiar with the project who will be able to more quickly answer your
questions.

## My operating system is (include version):
Ubuntu 16.04.4 LTS (xenial)


## I installed Certbot with (certbot-auto, OS package manager, pip, etc):
apt

## I ran this command and it produced this output:
```sh
$ certbot certificates
...
The following renewal configuration files were invalid:
  /etc/letsencrypt/renewal/mail.test.example.com.conf
```

## Certbot's behavior differed from what I expected because:
1. It should have told me *why* it was invalid -- the debug output for the renewal clearly explains that the files were expected to be symlinks.
1. It shouldn't say that the `configuration file is invalid`, it should say that the `configuration is invalid` -- the file itself is totally reasonable and anyone using `diff` to compare configuration files will be barking up the wrong tree.

## Here is a Certbot log showing the issue (if available):
###### Logs are stored in `/var/log/letsencrypt` by default. Feel free to redact domains, e-mail and IP addresses as you see fit.

2018-07-23 09:37:06,826:WARNING:certbot.renewal:Renewal configuration file /etc/letsencrypt/renewal/mail.test.example.com.conf is broken. Skipping.
```

see also #2550

issue_title: add a test git repo tarball into scriptworker/test/data
issue_body:
Currently [two of our tests](https://github.com/mozilla-releng/scriptworker/blob/4d6fb6f67ef8f94676b41755f3c325e30d87cca9/scriptworker/test/test_gpg.py#L593-L605), `test_get_git_revision` and `test_get_latest_tag`, use the current scriptworker git repo for tests.

This is poor for at least two reasons:
1. because we don't know what each person's git repo status will be when they run the tests, we have to run another git command to find the latest revision or the latest tag.  It isn't deterministic.
2. it isn't possible to run the tests without the .git subdir, e.g. from a pip tarball.  The dockerfile currently pushes the .git subdir to the container so we can run unit tests.

Instead, let's

a. create a test git repo, with a few revisions, and a few tags.
b. tar it up and add it to `scriptworker/test/data/`
c. change those two tests to extract that tarball into a tmpdir, and then running the tests against that tmpdir.

we can then have a deterministic latest tag and latest revision.

## Problem Definition

PR #2040 introduced a regression:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/35613178/37934945-8a6f2e8e-3115-11e8-8ae8-a134a884db80.png)
Any ideas what am I missing?

## Problem Definition

Connection manager (CM) is used to automatically join the token network using a certain amount of funds. It will automatically create payment channels with random peers and will distribute the funds equally across the channels.

There're several problems with the implementation
- the peers to create the channels with are chosen randomly, regardless of their state. Online nodes should be preferred instead.
- current implementation could use some refactoring in regard to parallel `connect()` calls. Now it just serializes parallel operations with a lock, which can sometime lead to confusing results.
- the REST api allows multiple calls to `connect()` method, even when the network channel join/deposit cycle is in progress. In such case it would be appropriate to return E409 if the network join is in progress. A GET request to `/api/1/connections/<token_address>` should return status of connection manager as well

To play devils advocate, I think that connection manager is an ideal candidate to remove from Raiden core - anything it does could be done in a couple of REST calls, either sent by a cli tool or a webUI.

I think the for loop is supposed to be:

```for key in range(len(self.slices)):```

issue_title: Replacing our progress bar with tqdm
issue_body:
We have done a lot to use progress bar in a multiprocessing setting, but [tqdm](https://github.com/tqdm/tqdm) seem to [also have such a problem](https://github.com/tqdm/tqdm/issues/285). However, it makes sense to use tqdm, which is supposed to have less overhead (at least they take overhead seriously) than our progress bar.

Another side of the story is 'it is not broken, do not fix it'.

Before we come up with a real web console (`sos status -q --console`), it seems to be easy to develop a `%tasklist` (or `%listtask`) magic. This magic will get all the tasks, for example, from a queue, and the icons will be displayed as if they were submitted from some sos scripts. Users can check the details of the tasks by clicking the icon, stop/resume tasks by clicking the status icon...

I would say this is almost as good as  a web console ... even more so if we make `%taskinfo` update itself from the side panel.

The public jupyter server has not been running well. Upon close inspection, the server is running on a tiny VM with only 1 G of RAM so it might have been swapping. I upgraded the VM but I am getting some proxy error. Upgrading Jupyter inside the VM produces some authentication error.

Perhaps @jma7 can have a look and fix the issue but it might be easier just to shut off the service.

Note that I have another ec2 instance of the server at http://ec2-34-226-117-10.compute-1.amazonaws.com:8000/

issue_title: Normalize() transform has undocumented side effect
issue_body:
It seems like [F.normalize()](https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/master/torchvision/transforms/functional.py#L168) does all of its operations in-place. 

I feel like this should either be very clearly documented or be hidden behind a flag.

This really bit me when I was just sending some batch images to Visdom but my denormalization function was silently denormalizing my batch before doing a forward pass on the network. It was hidden to me until now and surfaces because a copy disappeared when I removed all Variable() wrappers.

issue_title: Backend for Azure Active Directory B2C
issue_body:
There's Azure service called [AD B2C](https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/services/active-directory-b2c/https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/services/active-directory-b2c/) for IAM. The differences between Azure AD and Azure AD B2C are different API endpoints and request/response parameters. Therefore there's a necessity to develop new backend for this service.

Functionality to be developed is common: login, sign up, logout and token refresh.

This issue is a copy of https://github.com/python-social-auth/social-app-django/issues/61 but I think this is the correct repo for it.

Would it be possible to detect that `NO_COLOR` (http://no-color.org/) is set and not produce colored output in this case? 

I have bad eyes and use high-contrast black-on-white color schemes. Short of always remembering to invoke pulumi with the `-c never` option, there's no way to make the output uniformly legible for people who need high contrast to work.

Attached are screenshots that illustrate
1) running pulumi in my terminal
2) running `pulumi update -c never` and still getting purple output on the screen.

![pulumi-in-white-terminal](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/344774/41248783-deebf0e0-6d66-11e8-9366-f777f01c42b2.png)
![pulumi-in-white-terminal-color-never](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/344774/41248784-df04fb9e-6d66-11e8-9f5e-40b78c10c75f.png)

Today, the default values for `provider` and `protect` are set globally.  Instead, when not provided explicitly, the values for these should be inferred by walking up the parent chain until an explicit setting for the corresponding property is discovered - and if none is found, the global default can be applied (as though it was the setting on the stack resource that is the root of the tree).

For `provider`, we will allow components to specify a `providers: {[key: string]: ProviderResource}` which is a map from provider names (`aws`, `gcp`, `kubernetes`, etc.) that are registered with the Pulumi engine to provider instances (created via `new aws.Provider(...)`).  For resource created inside such a component, if they specify the component as their `parent`, then when looking up which provider instance to use - if one is not explicitly provided at the `new aws.Resource(...)` site - the corresponding provider will be looked up in the `providers` bag of the component, and if found, used as the provider.  This will repeat up the component parent pointer stack.  If not found, the ambient provider instance (based on external config) will be used.

For `protect`, the default will be determined directly from the `protect` property on the parent.  So all children (including indirect children) of a component marked as `protect: true` will default to being protected unless they override `protect: false` within the component (which itself should be a *very* rare tool for a component author to use!).  (Notably, this makes these fields more explicitly three-valued.

This is a general technique which should be applied in the future to all options exposed on `ResourceOptions`, so that component authors do not by default need to forward any settings to children - all they should need to do in the normal case is pass `{ parent: this }`.

issue_title: Build a package and get it upstreamed into Debian
issue_body:
Currently livestreamer is available as a package which can be easily installed on Debian/Ubuntu, this is not the case for streamlink. If we want to get this packaged up and ready for the next release we need to move quickly. as @javiercantero mentions [here]https://github.com/chrippa/livestreamer/issues/1427#issuecomment-249653892) they'll be freezing the the release in the beginning of January. While I'm familiar with building RPMs and am okay at building debs I'm not very familiar with building debs that go into the upstream repo. If we have anyone who is an expert working on the project I would love to get your help with this. I don't think we'll be able to get this sort of coverage (http://docs.livestreamer.io/install.html#linux-and-bsd-packages), but having it be available for at least Debian and Ubuntu would be nice.

There is some existing stuff related to the builds that chrippa put in place (https://packages.debian.org/sid/video/livestreamer), but I think we'll have to do some clean up to get everything prepared.

 Is this something that we want to do? Or should we wait on a package till we've rebuilt the CLI, then hope we can get it in at a later date?

issue_title: nrfutil dfu ble on Ubuntu 18.04 does not complete
issue_body:
I'm trying to verify DFU update capability in this configuration:
* Ubuntu 18.04 64-bit host
* nRF5-SDK 12.3.0 examples/dfu/bootloader_secure on target with S130 2.0.1
* nrfutil 3.5.1 with connectivity_2.0.1_115k2_with_s130_2.0.1.hex installed on the programmer

Invoking with:

    nrfutil dfu ble \
     -ic NRF51 \
     -pkg /tmp/app_dfu_package.zip \
     -p /dev/ttyUSB0 \
     -a E2:82:FE:1D:97:AC \
     -n DfuTarg

the debug output on the target shows the connection is made, and data starts being stored, but then the programmer terminates with:

```
  File "/home/pab/.local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/nordicsemi/__main__.py", line 838, in ble
    dfu.dfu_send_images()
  File "/home/pab/.local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/nordicsemi/dfu/dfu.py", line 129, in dfu_send_images
    self._dfu_send_image(self.manifest.application)
  File "/home/pab/.local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/nordicsemi/dfu/dfu.py", line 102, in _dfu_send_image
    self.dfu_transport.send_firmware(data)
  File "/home/pab/.local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/nordicsemi/dfu/dfu_transport_ble.py", line 549, in send_firmware
    response['crc'] = self.__stream_data(data=data, crc=response['crc'], offset=i)
  File "/home/pab/.local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/nordicsemi/dfu/dfu_transport_ble.py", line 619, in __stream_data
    self.dfu_adapter.write_data_point(map(ord, to_transmit))
  File "/home/pab/.local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/nordicsemi/dfu/dfu_transport_ble.py", line 332, in write_data_point
    self.adapter.write_cmd(self.conn_handle, DFUAdapter.DP_UUID, data)
  File "/home/pab/.local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pc_ble_driver_py/ble_adapter.py", line 366, in write_cmd
    raise e
pc_ble_driver_py.exceptions.NordicSemiException: Failed to ble_gattc_write. Error code: 3
```

Failure generally occurs after one or two, sometimes as many as 9, successful 256 By transfers.  The maximum I've been able to successfully transfer is 16 KiBy.  After failure I have to reset the target board to try again.

My hypothesis is that `3` is `NRF_ERROR_INTERNAL` and indicates a checksum error transferring data from the Linux host into the programmer.  I'm working to verify that, but hope somebody might provide further insight.

For completeness, I can't talk to the programmer at all when using PCA10031, PCA10028, or PCA10040 (with v5 connectivity), but I believe that to be either a CDC-ACM driver issue or a bug in `pc-ble-driver`.  In the steps above I'm using a Waveshare BLE400 which uses a more reliable serial driver on the Linux side.

issue_body:
Howdy ho!

Test fails with:

    Expected:
        (37.386012999999998, -122.082932)

        (37.386013, -122.082932)

It looks to be fixed in git. Please consider a new release and update to pypi.

Thanks!
